# Bootstrapping the filter {#sec-bootstrap}

The experiments so far used a pretrained CLIP model to select the aligned pairs. As a
*cognitive* proposal that is a non-starter: a child has no pretrained CLIP. The CLIP
filter is standing in for exactly the grounded knowledge whose *acquisition* we are trying
to explain. So the real question is whether alignment can be **bootstrapped from the
learner's own developing word knowledge** — the move @vong2024's symbolic ancestor
[@frank2009] made by jointly inferring the lexicon and the per-situation *referential
intent* (which objects the speaker means). Better guesses about word meaning sharpen the
guess about what is being referred to, and vice versa.

In our terms: replace the external filter with a **self-estimated** soft alignment that
co-evolves with the embeddings. That is an EM loop — the neural reincarnation of FGT's
Gibbs sampler over the intent variable that CVCL discarded (CVCL assumes *every*
co-occurring pair is a positive). The modern name for this problem is *noisy
correspondence* learning [@huang2021ncr], and the reason it can work in principle is the
**memorization effect**: networks fit consistent, repeated structure before they memorize
noise, so early-training similarity is itself a weak alignment signal — no external model
required.

## E0 — the naive bootstrap does not ignite {#sec-e0}

The simplest version (`train_boot.py`): warm up on all pairs, then iterate — score each
pair by the model's *own* cross-modal cosine, fit a two-component mixture to those scores
to get a soft "aligned" weight, and retrain weighted by that belief. CLIP is never used in
training; it is kept only as a held-out yardstick (does the endogenous weight recover the
CLIP-aligned pairs?). We ran a {within-kid, across-kid} × {data size} grid.

| pool | size | unweighted | **bootstrap** | ρ(weight, CLIP) |
|------|------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| across | 20k / 60k / 140k | 28.6 / 29.4 / 33.0 | 28.9 / 30.8 / 32.2 | 0.02–0.04 |
| within | 20k / 60k / 110k | 26.8 / 28.9 / 32.2 | 26.1 / 29.2 / 31.2 | 0.06–0.07 |

*(CLIP-oracle toplines: across 46.7, within 34.9; chance 25.)*

Three findings:

1. **No ignition.** The bootstrap ≈ the unweighted baseline everywhere (Δ within ±1.3).
   The endogenous weight is essentially uncorrelated with true alignment (ρ ≈ 0.02–0.07),
   so it selects near-randomly and adds nothing.
2. **Data amount helps the *unweighted* baseline, not the bootstrap.** Accuracy rises with
   pool size (28.6 → 33.0), but the bootstrap tracks it — more data does not create the
   cold-start toehold.
3. **Within vs. across dissociates as predicted.** Within-kid gives a consistently
   *stronger* (if still weak) toehold (ρ ≈ 0.07 vs ≈ 0.03) — one child's consistent world
   is easier to bootstrap from — but a much *lower* ceiling (oracle 34.9 vs 46.7), because
   one child's exemplars are fewer and less diverse. Easier start, lower ceiling.

**Diagnosis.** At BabyView's alignment floor (~5–13% of pairs aligned) the memorization
toehold is too faint: after warming up on ~88% misaligned pairs, the model cannot score
aligned pairs above misaligned ones from a single frame's global embedding. Noisy-
correspondence methods succeed at 20–50% noise; this is far past that. A *stronger*
endogenous signal is needed — which motivates the next experiment.

## E1 — region-level bootstrapping {#sec-e1}

If a whole cluttered frame is too weak a cue, ask a sharper question: *is there some
region in the frame that matches these words?* We embed a coarse region grid (frozen
DINOv2 patch tokens pooled to 4×4, plus the global CLS token) and let an utterance match
its **best region** (multiple-instance learning). The same max-region similarity becomes
the endogenous alignment score for the EM loop. This needs no object detector and no CLIP,
and it implements the "which frames *and* which regions" double-sparsity idea directly.

Region-MIL cleanly separates two things that E0 had conflated — the *representation* and
the *self-supervision signal*.

| region-MIL | across | within | vs CLS |
|------------|--------|--------|--------|
| oracle (aligned pairs given) | **49.9** | 39.4 | +3.2 / +4.5 |
| unweighted baseline | 34.8 | 34.1 | +1.8 / +1.9 |
| bootstrap (best) | 34.3 | 31.4 | ≈ |
| bootstrap ρ(weight, CLIP) | 0.035 | 0.06 | ≈ E0 |

**Region attention raises the achievable ceiling** (oracle 46.7 → 49.9) and lifts the
unweighted baseline — matching an utterance to its best region is a better representation,
consistent with the object-crop result of @sec-d. **But the bootstrap still does not
ignite:** the max-region alignment score is no better than the whole-frame cosine at
telling aligned pairs from misaligned ones (ρ ≈ 0.03–0.07, unchanged). A stronger
*representation* did not buy a stronger *self-supervision signal*.

The lesson: the toehold problem is about the **scoring function**, not the visual
features. The gap between the unweighted region model (34.8) and its oracle (49.9) — ~15
points — is exactly what a working bootstrap could recover. The next experiments therefore
target the E-step score itself: a **base-rate correction** (E8) that discounts
ever-present regions, and a **language prior** (E2) that trusts utterances containing
words the model already grounds.

## E2 / E8 — sharpening the E-step {#sec-e2e8}

If a stronger *representation* didn't fix the bootstrap, maybe the *scoring function* can.
Two ideas, both fully self-supervised: a **base-rate correction** (E8) that subtracts each
region's generic salience so ever-present regions stop winning, and a **language prior**
(E2) that up-weights utterances containing words the model already grounds (function words
self-exclude — no POS).

| variant (region-MIL boot) | pool | best 4AFC | ρ(weight, CLIP) |
|---------------------------|------|-----------|-----------------|
| language prior | within-110k | 32.6 | **0.095** |
| language prior | across-140k | 34.9 | 0.059 |
| distinctiveness | across-140k | 34.6 | 0.013 |
| distinctiveness + language | across-140k | **35.1** | 0.048 |

The **language prior helps most** — it roughly doubles the weight–alignment correlation
within-kid (0.06 → 0.095), while distinctiveness alone barely moves it. Best accuracy
edges to ~35, just above the unweighted region baseline (34.8) but still far from the
oracle (49.9). So: **the language side carries more bootstrappable signal than the visual
side** — an utterance's words are a better clue to whether a moment is referential than
the frame's regions are — but neither, instantaneously, is enough to ignite. The next
experiment gives the signal *memory*: accumulate a word→region prototype across situations.

## E5 — curriculum: bootstrap in one world, then transfer {#sec-e5}

E0 found that within-kid data gives the strongest (if faint) toehold — one child's
consistent world is easier to bootstrap from. Does that partial knowledge *transfer*? We
bootstrap on a single child (region-MIL + prototypes + language prior, shared vocabulary),
then continue the same model on the pooled corpus.

| run | best 4AFC | warm-up (pre-EM) | ρ(weight, CLIP) |
|-----|-----------|------------------|-----------------|
| across, from scratch (control) | 35.5 | 33.2 | 0.02 |
| within-kid (stage 1) | 34.2 | 33.6 | 0.07 |
| **across, initialised from stage 1** | **37.6** | 36.2 | 0.01 |

Curriculum gives the **best bootstrap of the night — 37.6**, +2.1 over the matched
from-scratch control. But the benefit is an **initialisation effect, not filter ignition**:
the pooled model already sits at 36.2 *before any EM rounds*, purely because it inherited
word meanings learned in the child's consistent world, and the weight–alignment
correlation stays ~0. The within-kid model learns transferable structure unsupervised; the
alignment *filter* still never ignites.

## Synthesis: what bootstrapping can and can't do here {#sec-boot-synth}

![Best 4AFC across the bootstrap mechanisms (across-kid, held-out S00360001; chance 25,
dotted line = region-MIL oracle 49.9). Every self-supervised filter lands in the 32–38
band near the unweighted baseline; only curriculum transfer (E5) and the region-MIL
representation move the needle.](figures/fig_bootstrap.png){#fig-boot width=90%}

Across six mechanisms — global cosine (E0), region-MIL (E1), a language prior (E2), a
base-rate correction (E8), cross-situational prototypes (E9), and a curriculum (E5) — the
self-supervised alignment *filter* never ignites: the endogenous weight stays essentially
uncorrelated with true alignment (ρ ≤ 0.1), and bootstrap accuracy stays pinned near the
unweighted baseline (~34–38), far from the region-MIL oracle (49.9).

Two things *do* help, and neither is a discovered filter:

- **Better representation.** Matching an utterance to its best *region* raises the ceiling
  (oracle 46.7 → 49.9) and the whole operating range by ~2 points.
- **Transfer.** Bootstrapping in one child's consistent world and carrying that lexicon to
  the pooled corpus is the single best recipe (37.6).

Two directional signals recur at every step: the **language side is more bootstrappable
than the visual side** (an utterance's words are a better cue to referential-ness than the
frame's regions), and **within-kid consistency beats the pooled corpus** for getting
started.

The honest conclusion is a negative with teeth. At BabyView's referential floor (~5–13% of
frame–utterance pairs aligned), **co-occurrence alone is too sparse for a learner to
*discover* which moments are aligned** — the memorization toehold that self-training relies
on never forms. This is not a failure of the specific tricks; five independent scoring
functions agree. It is evidence that the missing ingredient is exactly what a child has and
this data stream does not: **external structure that supplies reference** — a caregiver's
gaze and pointing, joint attention, prosodic highlighting. In the FGT framing, the
referential intent $I_s$ cannot be inferred from $(words, pixels)$ alone at this noise
level; it has to be *cued*. That reframes the modeling target: not "learn to filter," but
"learn to read the social signals that make the aligned moments findable."

That reframing invites two concrete questions, which the rest of the chapter answers with
numbers: *how good* would an alignment cue have to be to ignite the loop, and *is any such
cue actually recoverable* from this stream?

## How much information would ignite it? {#sec-titrate}

The six mechanisms all put the same question to the data and get the same answer — the
endogenous signal is too weak — but they do not say *how* weak, relative to what would be
enough. We can put a number on the target by replacing the self-estimated alignment score
with a **synthetic cue of controlled quality**: a rank-Gaussian corruption of the held-out
CLIP labels with a tunable Spearman correlation $\rho$ to the truth. Sweeping $\rho$ holds
the model, data, and training loop fixed and varies only the informativeness of the
alignment signal — a direct titration of the target.

*How* the cue is used turns out to matter as much as its quality. A **soft** cue enters as
a graded per-pair weight in the contrastive loss: every pair still trains, scaled by how
aligned the cue believes it is. A **hard** cue is thresholded — keep the top fraction (here
12%, the aligned base rate) as positives, drop the rest to a floor. The hard version is the
faithful analogue of the Chapter 3 CLIP filter, which keeps `clip > 0.24` and discards the
rest.

| cue quality $\rho$ | resulting filter precision | 4AFC |
|--------------------|----------------------------|------|
| 0.1 | 0.16 | 32.5 |
| 0.3 | 0.24 | 34.6 |
| 0.5 | 0.35 | 38.0 |
| 0.7 | 0.49 | 39.5 |
| 1.0 | 1.00 | 41.7 |

*(Hard-gate mode; unweighted baseline 34.8, region-MIL oracle 49.9. The gate ceiling of
41.7 at $\rho = 1$ falls short of the plain oracle because it still leaks a 0.05 weight
floor onto negatives and runs the EM regime rather than clean contrastive training on the
aligned set.)*

Three things follow.

**There is an ignition threshold, near $\rho \approx 0.3$–$0.5$.** Below it — a cue that
picks pairs barely above the base rate — hard filtering is useless or actively *harmful*:
at $\rho = 0.1$ the gate keeps a near-random 12% subset and drops *below* baseline, having
discarded most of the usable data for no gain in purity. Above it, accuracy climbs. In
precision terms the cue must enrich the aligned base rate by **2–3×** (12% → ~25–35%) before
it buys anything.

**A soft cue cannot substitute for a hard one.** Used as a graded weight, even a *perfect*
cue ($\rho = 1.0$) reaches only 37.8 — short of the 41.7 the same cue delivers when
thresholded, and further still from the oracle. Contrastive learning is dragged by the
pairs it does not fully exclude; a confident belief that a pair is misaligned must be
*acted on* (the pair dropped), not merely down-weighted. Any real cue therefore has to be
committed to a discrete decision, not folded in as a soft prior.

**The loop does not amplify a toehold.** A third mode seeds *only* the warm-up with the cue
and then hands control back to the endogenous EM. If self-training had any amplification
gain — the "memorisation toehold" that noisy-correspondence methods rely on — a seeded
start would compound into a better endogenous filter. It does not: the seeded runs decay
straight back to the unweighted baseline (34.8), because once EM recomputes weights from the
model's own $\rho \le 0.1$ scores the injected signal washes out. This is the mechanism
behind every negative result above. The bootstrap never stalled for want of a lucky start;
it stalls because there is no gain in the loop to turn a weak signal into a strong one. The
alignment information has to be *supplied and maintained* from outside, at $\rho \gtrsim
0.3$.

## Can we recover the cues children use? {#sec-cue-audit}

The titration sets a bar; the question is whether any signal actually present in the
BabyView stream clears it. Children have cues that this corpus's pixels and words do not
obviously carry — a caregiver's gaze and pointing, the prosody of child-directed speech —
and some machine-readable proxies are computable now. We score each with the same yardstick
used throughout: its Spearman correlation with held-out CLIP alignment.

From the person and object detections (available for 38% of pairs) we take **hands**
(present, count, near-contact with an object), **people** (presence and apparent closeness
via box area), and scene clutter. From adjacent 1fps frame embeddings we take **head
stability** (cosine between neighbouring frames — a proxy for the head motion the IMU also
records). From the audio we take **prosody** over each utterance window: the energy and
pitch dynamics that mark child-directed speech.

| cue | $\rho$ vs CLIP alignment |
|-----|--------------------------|
| hands (present / count / contact) | ~0.005 |
| people, scene clutter | −0.04 to −0.10 |
| head stability | −0.156 |
| *vision cues combined* | *0.136 (AUC 0.539)* |
| prosody: pitch range | 0.107 |
| prosody: energy range | 0.103 |
| *prosody combined* | *0.147 (AUC 0.607)* |

The result is a clean negative with one instructive exception. **Hands — the most intuitive
social cue — carry essentially no alignment information** ($\rho \approx 0.005$), for a
structural reason: in infant egocentric video a hand is almost always in frame (the child's
own, or a caregiver's), so hand *presence* cannot separate referential from non-referential
moments, and hand–object contact from coarse boxes at 1fps is too blunt to say *which*
object is meant. Head stability is the only visual cue with any signal, and it is weak and
*negative* — stiller moments are slightly *less* aligned, the opposite of the held-object
intuition.

**Prosody is the best channel**: its cues are all positive and it separates aligned pairs
better than vision (AUC 0.607 vs 0.539), echoing the language-over-vision asymmetry that
recurred through the bootstrap experiments. The child-directed-speech emphasis proxies
(pitch and energy range) reach $\rho \approx 0.10$ from an independent, language-side
channel. (Utterance *duration* correlates more strongly, $\rho = 0.17$, but partly as an
artefact: the CLIP label is a maximum over the utterance's frames, so longer utterances
score higher mechanically.)

But no channel clears the bar. The best combination of real cues reaches $\rho \approx
0.14$ — above the best *endogenous* bootstrap signal (0.095), so social cues do carry more
than self-supervision, yet still only a third to a half of the $\rho \gtrsim 0.3$ the
titration demands. The cues we can currently read are real but too weak, by a factor of two
to three.

## What is missing, quantified {#sec-missing}

Together the two analyses close the Chapter 3 argument with a number. Alignment filtering
works when the filter is good; a *self-supervised* filter never gets good, because the loop
has no amplification gain; and the *social* cues that could supply the filter from outside —
as they do for a child — are, in their currently machine-readable forms, two to three times
too weak to ignite it. The negative result of the six mechanisms is therefore not a
modelling shortfall awaiting a seventh trick. It is a measurement: at BabyView's referential
floor, the alignment signal available to a passive learner — from co-occurrence, from hands,
from head motion, even from prosody — sits below the threshold at which any filter, learned
or given, begins to help.

The gap is specific, and it points somewhere concrete. Every cue that failed measured
*presence* — is a hand in frame, is the scene still. The cue the joint-attention literature
says matters is *direction*: the vector from a caregiver's gaze or pointing arm to the
object being named. Bounding boxes cannot represent a direction; that is exactly the
information they discard. Whole-body pose — face landmarks for head orientation,
shoulder–elbow–wrist for a pointing ray — can, and pose estimates already exist for roughly
55% of this corpus's training pairs. Whether a gaze- or point-derived cue crosses the $\rho
\approx 0.3$ line is the one measurement this chapter leaves open — and the titration turns
it from a hope into a precise, falsifiable target: not "add social cues," but "find a cue
worth $\rho \geq 0.3$, or show that none exists in this stream."
